name: Dynamic Programming
coreIntuition: Dynamic programming is NOT a technique you 'apply' — it's an observation about problem structure. When a problem has **overlapping subproblems** (the same smaller problem is solved repeatedly) and **optimal substructure** (the optimal solution is built from optimal solutions to smaller pieces), you can store and reuse subproblem results instead of recomputing them. DP doesn't make problems easier — it makes the recursive solution you already have faster.
mentalModel: Start by thinking recursively: 'How can I break this problem into smaller versions of itself?' Write the recurrence relation first — don't think about tables or bottom-up. Once you have a correct recursive solution, DP is just adding a cache. The table is an optimization, not the starting point. Think of DP as **smart recursion** — you're not learning a new algorithm, you're making recursion efficient by remembering what you already computed.
whenToUse: The problem asks for an optimal value (minimum cost, maximum profit, number of ways)
You can define the answer in terms of answers to smaller subproblems (recurrence relation)
The same subproblems appear multiple times when you draw the recursion tree
The problem involves making sequential choices where each choice affects future options
Greedy doesn't work because you need to consider multiple paths

thinkingFramework.step: 1. Define the state — what parameters uniquely identify a subproblem?
thinkingFramework.detail: This is the single most important step. Ask: 'What information do I need to make the next decision?' For knapsack: (item index, remaining capacity). For LIS: (ending index). The state IS your recurrence parameter — get this wrong and everything else falls apart.
Express dp[state] in terms of dp[smaller states]. This IS the core logic. For 0/1 knapsack: dp[i][w] = max(dp[i-1][w], dp[i-1][w-weight[i]] + value[i]). Don't think about code yet — write the math.
What's the smallest subproblem you can solve directly? For knapsack: dp[0][w] = 0 (no items). For fibonacci: dp[0] = 0, dp[1] = 1. Base cases are where the recursion stops.
If you go bottom-up, you need to fill the table so that every cell you reference is already computed. Follow the direction of the recurrence: if dp[i] depends on dp[i-1], iterate i from 0 upward.
If dp[i] only depends on dp[i-1], you don't need the full table — just two rows (or even one). This is a mechanical optimization after you have the correct recurrence.

commonTraps.trap: Trying to find the 'DP pattern' instead of thinking about the problem
commonTraps.howToAvoid: Don't ask 'is this a knapsack problem?' Ask 'what's the recurrence?' Every DP problem has a unique recurrence — learn to derive it, not to pattern-match.

commonTraps.trap: Wrong state definition — missing a parameter or adding unnecessary ones
commonTraps.howToAvoid: Test your state by asking: 'Given these parameters, can I make the next decision without any other information?' If yes, the state is complete. If no, you're missing a parameter.

commonTraps.trap: Confusing top-down and bottom-up, and not knowing when to use which
commonTraps.howToAvoid: Top-down (memoized recursion) is easier to write and think about — start there. Bottom-up is an optimization for when you need space efficiency or iterative performance. They compute the same thing.

commonTraps.trap: Forgetting that DP requires optimal substructure — not just overlapping subproblems
commonTraps.howToAvoid: Verify: does the optimal solution to the full problem actually contain optimal solutions to subproblems? If a locally suboptimal choice might lead to a globally optimal solution, DP might not work (and you may need a different approach).

interviewVariantStrategy: Interviewers twist DP problems by: (1) **Changing the objective** — 'count the number of ways' vs 'find the minimum cost' vs 'is it possible'. Same recurrence structure, different combination operation (addition for counting, min/max for optimization, OR for feasibility). (2) **Adding dimensions** — 'now you have a cooldown period' adds a state parameter. (3) **Disguising as a different problem** — 'longest palindromic subsequence' is really 'LCS of string with its reverse'. The defense: always derive the recurrence from scratch. Ask 'what choices do I have at step i?' and 'how does each choice affect the remaining problem?' — this works for ANY DP problem, even ones you've never seen.

resources.title: MIT 6.006 — Dynamic Programming (Erik Demaine)
resources.why: The gold standard DP lecture — teaches the SRTBOT framework for systematically solving any DP problem

resources.title: Aditya Verma — DP Playlist (Hindi)
resources.why: 48 videos building DP understanding from first principles — the recursion-tree approach is excellent

resources.title: AtCoder DP Contest — Educational DP Problems
resources.why: 26 carefully designed DP problems (A-Z) that progressively teach every important DP pattern. The best practice set that exists.

resources.title: CP-Algorithms — Dynamic Programming
resources.why: Reference-quality explanations with mathematical rigor for each DP technique
